findings.title: Marcus chapter endings default to interior reflection or capped-pen gesture instead of image/action/dialogue
findings.description: The style guide states explicitly: 'End on an image, an action, or a piece of dialogue — not on a summarizing reflection.' Three of Marcus's four Act 1 chapters violate this rule; the pattern continues into Act 2. The capped-pen gesture appears twice in Act 1 (ch03 and ch06), and ch09 closes on a decision-to-not-think that is a summarizing emotional state, not an observed detail. The same manuscript demonstrates the correct technique repeatedly in Eira's chapter endings. This is a mode, not a one-off choice.

evidence.text: He put the cap on his pen and left the grade book open on the desk.
He sat for a while with the pen, and then he capped it and put it down.
He closed his eyes and lay still while the house settled around him in the cold, the furnace in its slow rhythm below, the January dark clear at the edge of the window, and did not think further.

findings.suggested_action: Audit all ten Marcus chapters against the style guide's ending rule. Replace every ending that closes on interior reflection or the capped-pen gesture with an image, an action, or a piece of dialogue. Use Eira's chapter endings as the in-manuscript model.

findings.title: Marcus's narration explicitly names and explains his displacement condition rather than demonstrating it
findings.description: Marcus's chapters contain three extended passages where his psychological condition is articulated in prose that is too explanatory for the character's established voice. Ch09's Duldung paragraph names and defines the concept at length, then applies it to Marcus's own situation explicitly. Ch12's heimisch meditation recycles the word seven-plus times in approximately thirty pages, becoming recursive rather than cumulative. Ch27's Ansässigkeit meditation extends approximately one paragraph past its emotional point into legal-bureaucratic etymology. The contrast with ch19 — where the same territory is approached obliquely through Schell's biography and Marcus's recognition in it — shows what these passages should do instead.

evidence.text: not acceptance, not welcome, not even the more reliable forms of indifference, but a space defined by someone else's tolerance, maintained at their discretion, sustained as long as it served their purposes to sustain it
heimisch meditation — word appears seven-plus times within approximately thirty pages, becoming recursive (what the word means, why it applies, why it doesn't apply, why the distinction matters)
Ansässigkeit meditation's final passage — legal-bureaucratic etymology runs one beat past the emotional point established by 'I think I have stopped moving'

findings.suggested_action: Cut or compress each passage so that Marcus's psychological state is approached through behavior, observation, and oblique analogy (as in ch19) rather than direct naming and analysis. The sentence that follows the Duldung paragraph — 'His name was on the spring schedule. That was a fact.' — is already the correct ending; move it forward and cut the apparatus.

findings.title: Eira's narration tips into analytical interior monologue, violating her established register
findings.description: The style guide is unambiguous: Eira 'avoids extended interior monologue' and moves 'from observation to inference without ornament.' Two chapters in Act 2 break this rule at length. Ch13's harbor-office scene tips from notation into Eira analyzing the shape of the coincidences in the register of a detective explaining the case. Ch16's post-inspection scene contains a four-paragraph internal review explicitly bounded by 'These were the boundaries of what she knew.' A third instance in ch16 has the narrator commenting on an implication the reader already holds. The technique ch20 uses — rendering the same psychological territory through the official addendum form and physical action — proves the alternatives are available within the manuscript.

evidence.text: What she had was a set of coincidences, and coincidences were what the record looked like when it was incomplete
Four-paragraph internal review of everything known and unknown, culminating in 'These were the boundaries of what she knew.'
He had taken a berth on a merchant ship running the Atlantic in wartime, which was not a safe thing to do.

findings.suggested_action: In ch13 and ch16, replace the analytical paragraphs with the technique demonstrated in ch20: render Eira's thinking through notation, official form, and physical action rather than explicit interior statement. The ch20 addendum is the proof of concept; apply its logic backward to the two earlier chapters.

findings.title: Hannah's narration drifts toward adult retrospective analytical voice in three chapters across all three acts
findings.description: The canon/characters.md file specifies that Hannah's narration avoids 'adult retrospective wisdom. She is seventeen; she does not yet know how this ends.' Three passages across Acts 1, 2, and 3 break this constraint in the same way: Hannah analyzes a concept (Heimweh, mündig, mündig again) with the precision of someone who has processed the experience rather than someone living inside it. The ch08 Heimweh passage is analytically sophisticated in a way that approaches Marcus's register. The ch14 mündig construction is a thematic summary in the rhythm of a classroom lecture. The ch23 mündig re-gloss explains a word to the reader nine chapters after it was planted, from a vantage Hannah does not yet have.

evidence.text: Two-paragraph analysis of Heimweh — 'And the ache did not end because the home it was aimed at was no longer accessible and maybe had never been quite real even when you lived in it, the way you sometimes don't fully see a place until you have left it'
But mündig was not about naming things. Mündig was about whose voice the naming came from. Who had the right to put the name in.
Paragraph beginning 'She thought about the word mündig' — re-glosses etymology ('having your own mouth, the state of speaking for oneself') nine chapters after ch14

findings.suggested_action: In ch08, trim the Heimweh analysis to its emotional core and remove the analytical apparatus. In ch14, cut the mündig-as-lecture paragraph to the moment of recognition; the word can carry its meaning without structured explication. In ch23, cut the mündig etymology paragraph entirely; 'She was sure it was hers' carries everything that passage is trying to say.

findings.title: Portland postmark appears as confirmed fact in Eira's log without being established in Ada Gile's dialogue
findings.description: In ch13, Ada Gile tells Eira that the general-delivery postcard was addressed to 'A. Schell,' contained a date and a time, and arrived the first week of October — but does not specify Portland as the postmark city. Eira's personal log entry in the same chapter records the Portland postmark as a confirmed fact. The Portland postmark then drives Eira's theory in both ch13 and ch16 (Macomber's Portland Navy district contract converging with a Portland postcard). This is either a fact established in an elided exchange (Ada mentions it between rendered sentences) or a genuine continuity gap in how the fact transferred from Ada to Eira's log. The canon/threads.md entry for T1 at ch13 describes the postcard only as 'a date and a time, nothing else,' which does not resolve the discrepancy.

evidence.text: Ada's rendered dialogue: postcard, general delivery, 'A. Schell,' a date and a time — postmark city not specified
Eira's notebook entry: 'A. Schell — general delivery — postcard — Portland postmark — early October' — Portland stated as confirmed
Eira uses Portland postmark as established fact when constructing her theory after Whitmore's visit

findings.suggested_action: Either add the Portland postmark to Ada Gile's rendered dialogue in ch13, or remove it from Eira's notebook entry and supply an alternative path by which the Portland connection is established (e.g., Eira checks the postal mark herself, or the detail arrives through another source).

findings.title: Retrospective flashback in ch26 placed incorrectly, interrupting the chapter's forward resolution
findings.description: The November retrospective in ch26's final third — Hannah watching from the kitchen window as her father and Karl speak in the November cold, Albrecht's hand on the counter afterward — is necessary material and well-executed. But it arrives after a meditation that should be settling forward toward Magda's returning speech, which is the chapter's real pivot (T5's movement toward resolution). Placed where it is, the flashback pulls backward at the moment the chapter should be completing its forward motion. The November timeframe of the flashback material also fits more naturally in ch25, where the chronology aligns.

evidence.text: Retrospective flashback (Albrecht and Karl speaking, twenty minutes, November cold, Albrecht's hand on counter) appears in chapter's final third, after Hannah's meditation on what Karl has and what she has, interrupting the approach to Magda's returning speech

findings.suggested_action: Move the flashback material to ch26's earlier section (as context for Hannah's understanding of what Karl's loss means specifically to her father) or relocate to ch25, where the November timeframe fits naturally and where the information would serve Eira's ambient chapter without disrupting the T5 resolution arc.

findings.title: Weak chapter endings and over-procedural opening in late Eira chapters
findings.description: Ch25 ends 'She went to bed' — deflation rather than rest. By the manuscript's own standards (chapter endings close on something with a forward edge, not a settling-back), this is below the line the book has set. Ch29's opening devotes two full beats to harbor-administration texture (arrival figures, buoy tender schedule, pending Lighthouse Service notice) before the Whitmore call, which is the chapter's structural weight. Given that Eira has been absent from chapters 26–28 (five months of narrative time), ch29 carries additional pressure; the slow procedural opening makes that pressure more costly.

evidence.text: Chapter closing line: 'She went to bed.'
First two pages: arrival figures, buoy tender schedule, pending Lighthouse Service notice — two full beats of harbor administration before the Whitmore call

findings.suggested_action: Find a forward-facing image or action to close ch25, replacing 'She went to bed'; the kettle-and-tea sequence that precedes it could be trimmed by one paragraph to arrive at a stronger closing image. Trim ch29's harbor-administration preamble to a single beat before the Whitmore call.

findings.title: V-Mail chapter (ch21) second-half coda dilutes its central material
findings.description: The V-Mail chapter's central material — the specific omissions Hannah makes from Rolf's letter, and the observation that 'everyone in this town was keeping something' — completes its work at the moment that line lands. The walk-home section that follows (the observation of Klein in Room 6, the return to the farm road, the sheep count) constitutes a coda that extends across approximately four paragraphs and repeats the chapter's conclusion without advancing it. The sheep count (seven, eight, nine — the herd complete except for one) is a nice touch, but by that point the chapter has made its point and is rehearsing it.

evidence.text: Walk-home section following 'everyone in this town was keeping something': observation of Klein in Room 6, second return to farm road, sheep count (seven, eight, nine) — approximately four paragraphs extending past the chapter's thematic completion

findings.suggested_action: Cut the walk-home section to two paragraphs; trust the 'everyone in this town was keeping something' line to land without the sheep-count coda. If the Klein-in-Room-6 observation is needed for thread continuity, one sentence suffices.

findings.title: Ch07 re-examines departure-entry territory already covered in ch04, adding pacing weight
findings.description: In ch04, Eira discovers the Macomber departure entry in her own handwriting and works through the basic question of how it could have appeared there. In ch07, she explicitly notes she 'had worked through these possibilities in November' and then works through them again at greater length across three substantial paragraphs. The ch07 treatment is deliberately deeper and has different consequences (it results in the personal log entry 'Inquiry?'). But the repeated territory adds pacing weight to a chapter that could arrive at the personal log entry more directly, trusting ch04's prior establishment.

evidence.text: Eira works through how the Macomber departure entry could have appeared in her own hand — three possibilities examined
'she had worked through these possibilities in November' — then re-examines them at greater length across three paragraphs before arriving at 'Inquiry?' in the personal log

findings.suggested_action: In ch07, trim the re-examination to a single paragraph of notation (she returns to the possibilities, finds no new entry into them, marks the log) and move directly to the personal-log entry. Trust ch04's prior work.
